elements.sent-2411-001: Department of Statistics, North Carolina State University.
elements.sent-2411-002: Untreated periodontitis causes inflammation within the supporting tissue of the teeth and can ultimately lead to tooth loss.
elements.sent-2411-003: Modeling periodontal outcomes is beneficial as they are difficult and time consuming to measure, but disparities in representation between demographic groups must be considered.
elements.sent-2411-004: There may not be enough participants to build group specific models and it can be ineffective, and even dangerous, to apply a model to participants in an underrepresented group if demographic differences were not considered during training.
elements.sent-2411-005: We propose an extension to RECaST Bayesian transfer learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our method jointly models multivariate outcomes, exhibiting significant improvement over the previous univariate RECaST method.
elements.sent-2411-007: Further, we introduce an online approach to model sequential data sets.
elements.sent-2411-008: Negative transfer is mitigated to ensure that the information shared from the other demographic groups does not negatively impact the modeling of the underrepresented participants.
elements.sent-2411-009: The Bayesian framework naturally provides uncertainty quantification on predictions.
elements.sent-2411-010: Especially important in medical applications, our method does not share data between domains.
elements.sent-2411-011: We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in both predictive performance and uncertainty quantification on simulated data and on a database of dental records from the HealthPartners Institute.
elements.sent-2411-012: The field of transfer learning has developed to solve machine learning problems where data or computational resources may be limited.
elements.sent-2411-013: Transfer learning leverages information from source domains where data are plentiful to aid in learning about related, resource scarce target domains.
elements.sent-2411-014: Importantly, while these domains are similar they are not necessarily the same.
elements.sent-2411-015: If the domains are different, transfer learning methods may incur {\em negative transfer where the inclusion of unrelated source information hinders the learning of the target task.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The Random Effect Calibration of Source to Target (RECaST) framework proposed in \cite{hickey2022transfer is a Bayesian transfer learning method.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The RECaST framework represents the similarity between the source and target as a Cauchy-distributed random effect.
elements.sent-2411-018: The posterior distribution of the random effect parameters is learned on the target data using a model built in the source domain.
elements.sent-2411-019: This learned posterior distribution is used to construct predictions and posterior predictive credible sets for unseen target data -- providing both prediction and uncertainty quantification without necessitating the access to the source data.
elements.sent-2411-020: The HealthPartners Institute at Minneapolis, Minnesota has collected a longitudinal, observational data set of their participants through routine dental checkups as described in \cite{guan2020bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The data consider demographic and dental features along with two outcomes that are indicators of periodontal disease.
elements.sent-2411-022: Certain racial groups are much less represented than others in the data.
elements.sent-2411-023: Ignoring this imbalance and using all of the data jointly to build a single model could result in poor predictive predictive performance and uncertainty quantification on underrepresented populations.
elements.sent-2411-024: We address this disparity by sharing information across related groups with multivariate online RECaST.
elements.sent-2411-025: The proposed methods enable us to model both outcomes jointly and to create better models for underrepresented racial groups.
elements.sent-2411-026: While transfer learning methods to model online or multivariate data exist, no others provide uncertainty quantification on the predictions.
elements.sent-2411-027: We extend the RECaST framework in two ways.
elements.sent-2411-028: First, we model multivariate outcomes whereas the original framework was limited to univariate outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-029: We provide two different multivariate random effect distributions to model the similarity between source and target.
elements.sent-2411-030: One uses a multivariate Cauchy distribution, a natural extension from the univariate Cauchy.
elements.sent-2411-031: The other incorporates the univariate Cauchy marginal distributions of the original RECaST framework with a multivariate Gaussian copula.
elements.sent-2411-032: Second, we propose an online method to share information between sequential target data sets while mitigating negative transfer.
elements.sent-2411-033: As we demonstrate in the data analysis, this online analysis can also be applied to data that are collected concurrently but modeled sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-034: The newly proposed methods retain many of the advantages of the original RECaST framework.
elements.sent-2411-035: Data privacy is maintained between all data sets.
elements.sent-2411-036: Only the source model, not the source data, is required when fitting the posterior distribution for the target data.
elements.sent-2411-037: When considering multiple target data sets, only the learned posterior distribution from previous targets are shared and not the data themselves.
elements.sent-2411-038: This property is especially important when working with sensitive data such as medical information.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, the framework remains agnostic to the specification of the source model, such that it can correspond to a parametric generalized linear model or a highly flexible neural network.
elements.sent-2411-040: The RECaST methods are evaluated on both predictive performance and posterior predictive coverage.
elements.sent-2411-041: We consider synthetic simulation studies that vary sample size, outcome correlation, and similarity between source and target data sets.
elements.sent-2411-042: Changes in these parameters represent different challenges of real data sets and enables us to isolate the effects that each parameter has on predictive performance and uncertainty quantification.
elements.sent-2411-043: Negative transfer is examined throughout the studies.
elements.sent-2411-044: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: In Section \ref{section: related work we discuss related work in transfer learning.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We provide a summary of the original RECaST framework that this work builds upon in Section \ref{section: recap .}
elements.sent-2411-047: We extend this framework in Section \ref{section: multivariate to model multivariate outcomes, providing two natural modeling choices.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In Section \ref{section: online , we develop an online extension to handle sequential target data sets.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We test these methods in Section \ref{section: simulation study through extensive simulation studies.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Section \ref{section: dental data analyzes the periodontal disease outcomes.}

elements.sent-2411-082: The online RECaST method that we propose uses a prior informed by previous target data sets while mitigating negative transfer.
elements.sent-2411-083: We begin with a summary of the RECaST framework from \cite{hickey2022transfer .}
elements.sent-2411-084: RECaST is a Bayesian transfer learning method for source and target data sets that share the same outcome and feature spaces but may have differences in feature-to-outcome mappings.
elements.sent-2411-085: It is scalable, requiring estimation of only 2-3 parameters, which is especially important when there are minimal target data.
elements.sent-2411-086: RECaST maintains data privacy, requiring only the fitted source model to be shared and not the source data.
elements.sent-2411-087: The estimation of a Bayesian posterior distribution naturally begets uncertainty quantification through posterior predictive credible intervals.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{ Multivariate Cauchy RECaST Posterior Predictive Sampling}\label{algorithm: multivariate cauchy RECaST prediction} Input: $\widetilde{\bx}_{T}$, an estimated posterior $p(\bdelta, \bGamma, \bSigma \mid \by_{T,1}, \dots \by_{T, n_{T}})$, and sample sizes $n_{\text{post}}$, $n_{\bbeta}$, and $n_{Y}$ Output: A sample of values from $\pi\big(\widetilde{\by}_{T} \mid \by_{T,1}, \dots, \by_{T,n_{T}}, \widehat{\bTheta}_{S} \big)$ \begin{algorithmic} \For{$i \gets 1$ to $n_{\text{post}}$} \State $\bdelta, \bGamma, \bSigma \gets \text{random} \big\{ p\big( \bdelta, \bGamma, \bSigma \mid \by_{T,1}, \dots, \by_{T,n_{T}}, \widehat{\btheta}_{S}\big) \big\}$ \For{$j \gets 1$ to $n_{\bbeta}$} \State $\widetilde{ \bbeta } \gets \text{random}\big\{ \text{Cauchy}_{m}(\bdelta, \bGamma) \big\}$ \For{$k \gets 1$ to $n_{\bY}$} \State $\widetilde{\bY}_{T} \gets \text{random}\big[ \mathcal{N}_{m}\big\{\widetilde{ \by }_{T} \mid \text{diag}(\widetilde{ \bbeta }) f(\widehat{ \bTheta }_{S}, \widetilde{ \bx }_{T}), \bSigma \big\}\big]$ \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{ Multivariate Copula RECaST Posterior Predictive Sampling}\label{algorithm: copula RECaST prediction} Input: $\widetilde{\bx}_{T}$, an estimated posterior $p(\delta_{1}, \dots , \delta_{m} , \gamma_{1}, \dots , \gamma_{m}, \bR, \bSigma \mid \by_{1}, \dots , \by_{n_{T}}, \widehat{\bTheta}_{S})$, and sample sizes $n_{\text{post}}$, $n_{\bbeta}$, and $n_{Y}$ Output: A sample of values from $\pi\big(\widetilde{\by}_{T} \mid \by_{T,1}, \dots, \by_{T,n_{T}}, \widehat{\bTheta}_{S} \big)$ \begin{algorithmic} \For{$i \gets 1$ to $n_{\text{post}}$} \State $\delta_{1}, \dots , \delta_{m} , \gamma_{1}, \dots , \gamma_{m}, \bR, \bSigma \gets \text{random} \big\{ p(\delta_{1}, \dots , \delta_{m} , \gamma_{1}, \dots , \gamma_{m}, \bR, \bSigma \mid \by_{1}, \dots , \by_{n_{T}}, \widehat{\bTheta}_{S}) \big\}$ \For{$j \gets 1$ to $n_{\bbeta}$} \State $\widetilde{ \beta }_{1} \gets \text{random}\big\{ \text{Cauchy}(\delta_{1}, \gamma_{1}) \big\}$ \State $\vdots$ \State $\widetilde{ \beta }_{m} \gets \text{random}\big\{ \text{Cauchy}(\delta_{m}, \gamma_{m}) \big\}$ \For{$k \gets 1$ to $n_{\bY}$} \State $\widetilde{\bY}_{T} \gets \text{random}\big[ \mathcal{N}_{m}\big\{\widetilde{ \by }_{T} \mid \widetilde{ \bB } f(\widehat{ \bTheta }_{S}, \widetilde{ \bx }_{T}), \bSigma \big\}\big]$ \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-170: We further build upon the RECaST framework to model multiple sequential target data sets.
elements.sent-2411-171: We address the problem of online transfer learning, with multiple target domains, each of which may be lacking in sample size or computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-172: As with previous RECaST methods, we operate under the assumption that the data are private and cannot be shared across sites, even between targets.
elements.sent-2411-173: We propose a sequential approach that includes a RECaST model built in one target domain to inform a new target domain.
elements.sent-2411-174: We motivate the problem with an example.
elements.sent-2411-175: A hospital has data from the past 10 years to build a model that reliably diagnoses whether a patient has the flu -- this will act as the source.
elements.sent-2411-176: Now the hospital wants to use that model on incoming patients for this year's flu season -- this target data set is $T_{1}$.
elements.sent-2411-177: The influenza virus itself, however, may have changed or evolved and the previously built model may not be directly applicable.
elements.sent-2411-178: So a RECaST transfer learning model is used to calibrate the source model to this year's target data.
elements.sent-2411-179: The following year's flu season will see more patients -- this target data set is $T_{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-180: The online RECaST method uses the source model and the $T_{1}$ model to build a model on the $T_{2}$ patients.
elements.sent-2411-181: It is possible, however, that the new flu strain is very different between $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ and that including this extra information would be harmful.
elements.sent-2411-182: This would be an example of negative transfer.
elements.sent-2411-183: Our online method incorporates source and previous target data sets while reducing the influence of negative transfer.
elements.sent-2411-184: The online RECaST method can be applied to the univariate and multivariate outcome cases.
elements.sent-2411-185: Take $\by_{T_{1 , 1 , \dots \by_{T_{1 , n_{T_{1 }$ to be a sample of $n_{T_{1 }$ outcomes from the first target, $T_{1}$, and $\by_{T_{2 , 1 , \dots \by_{T_{2 , n_{T_{2 }$ to be a sample of $n_{T_{2 }$ outcomes from the second target, $T_{2}$, with associated features $\bx_{T_{2 , 1 , \dots \bx_{T_{2 , n_{T_{2 }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Take $\bOmega$ to be the unknown parameters: in the multivariate Cauchy model $\bOmega = \{ \bdelta, \bGamma , \bSigma\}$ and in the multivariate Gaussian copula model $\bOmega = \{\delta_{1 , \dots , \delta_{m , \gamma_{1 , \dots , \gamma_{m , \bR , \bSigma \}$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-212: In this section we examine the performance of the multivariate and online RECaST methods through simulations on synthetic data.

elements.sent-2411-308: We consider two periodontal measurements measured in millimeters (mm): clinical attachment level (CAL) and pocked depth (PD).
elements.sent-2411-309: PD is the ``distance from the gingival margin to the base of the gingival sulcus or periodontal pocket" and CAL is the ``distance from the cemento-enamel junction (or another definite chosen landmark) to the base of the sulcus or periodontal pocket" \citep{page2007case .}
elements.sent-2411-310: These are both important measurements in diagnosing periodontitis.
elements.sent-2411-311: A general categorization of periodontitis is: {\em slight = 1 to 2 mm of CAL, {\em moderate = 3 to 4 mm of CAL, and {\em severe $\geq$ 5 mm of CAL \citep{periodontitis2015american .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-353: To summarize, all of the multivariate RECaST methods improve joint prediction of CAL and PD in underrepresented populations.
elements.sent-2411-354: The biggest improvements are seen on the Native American and Alaska Native population, which is the least represented population in the data.
elements.sent-2411-355: The online approaches give the most accurate predictions, demonstrating the importance of including related target data.
elements.sent-2411-356: All of the multivariate RECaST methods provided excellent joint coverage properties, which could provide clinicians both improved predictions and credible sets.
elements.sent-2411-357: Future work for this method includes considering multiple measurements per participant.
elements.sent-2411-358: Some dental data sets provide CAL and PD measurements for all of the teeth in a participant's mouth rather than a single averaged outcome \citep{guan2020bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-359: This could further expand into including modeling longitudinal data with multiple follow-up visits for each participant.
elements.sent-2411-360: With the inclusion of other dental measurements, the spatial relationship between teeth can also be used \citep{jhuang2020spatiotemporal .}
elements.sent-2411-361: Additionally, this multivariate framework can be applied to multiclass outcomes that are common for image classification \citep{deng2009 .}

Then take priors \delta \sim N(\mu_{\delta}, \sigma^{2}_{\delta}) \gamma \sim IG(a_{\gamma}, b_{\gamma}).\end{equation}.

Then take priors \delta \sim N(\mu_{\delta}, \sigma^{2}_{\delta}) \gamma \sim IG(a_{\gamma}, b_{\gamma}).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-369: Setting $\nu = 1$ corresponds to setting a multivariate Cauchy prior on $\bmu$.
elements.sent-2411-370: When there are $\ell$ target data sets, the marginal posterior of $\balpha$ is \begin{equation}p(\balpha \mid \by_{T_{\ell}, 1}, \dots \by_{T_{\ell}, n_{T_{\ell}}} ) \propto \pi(\balpha) \cdot \Big[ \alpha_{\ell} \cdot k_{\ell} + \sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}\alpha_{i}\cdot k_{i} \Big].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-371: Where $\pi(\balpha)$ is the prior on the vector $\balpha$, $\alpha_{\ell}$ is the weight associated with the uninformative prior term $k_{\ell}$ and $\alpha_{i}$ and $k_{i}$ are the weights associated and posterior distribution terms associated with $T_{i}$, respectively, for $i \in 1, \dots , \ell - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-372: The normalizing constant is \begin{equation}1 = c \cdot \int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \pi(\balpha) \cdot \Big\{ \Big(1-\sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}\alpha_{i}\Big) k_{\ell} + \sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}\alpha_{i} k_{i} \Big\} \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} = c \cdot \Big\{ k_{\ell} \int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \pi(\balpha) \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} + (k_{i} - k_{\ell}) \int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \pi(\balpha) \cdot \alpha_{i} \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} \Big\} = c \cdot \Big\{ k_{\ell} + \sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}(k_{i} - k_{\ell}) E_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{i}) \Big\} c = \Big\{ k_{\ell} + \sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}(k_{i} - k_{\ell}) E_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{i}) \Big\}^{-1}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-373: The posterior mean of $\alpha_{j}$ is \begin{equation}E(\alpha_{j} \mid \by_{T_{\ell}, 1}, \dots \by_{T_{\ell}, n_{T_{\ell}}} ) = c \cdot \int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \alpha_{j} \cdot \pi(\balpha) \cdot \Big[ \Big(1-\sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}\alpha_{i}\Big) k_{\ell} + \sum_{i=1}^{\ell-1}\alpha_{i} k_{i} \Big] \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} = c \cdot \Big\{ k_{\ell} \int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \alpha_{j} \cdot \pi(\balpha) \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} + (k_{j} - k_{\ell})\int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \alpha_{j}^{2} \cdot \pi(\balpha) \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} + \sum_{i=1, i\neq j}^{\ell-1} (k_{i} - k_{\ell}) \int_{\alpha_{1}} \dots \int_{\alpha_{\ell-1}} \alpha_{j} \cdot \alpha_{i} \cdot \pi(\balpha) \cdot \ d\alpha_{\ell-1} \dots d\alpha_{1} \Big\} = c \cdot \Big[ k_{\ell} \cdot E_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{j}) + (k_{j} - k_{\ell}) \Big\{E_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{j})^{2} + Var_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{j}) \Big\} + \sum_{i=1,i\neq j}^{\ell-1}(k_{i}-k_{j}) \Big\{ Cov_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{i}, \alpha_{j}) + E_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{i}) E_{\pi(\balpha)}(\alpha_{j}) \Big\} \Big].\end{equation}. \bibliographystyle{apalike.}
